text: in fact develop alongside these practices. While certainly open to individual choices, they are clearly still social phenomena. This is so, first and foremost, for practitioners of the sport, who even develop their own language and codes of behaviour. It also becomes clear from the fact that many bungee posts are placed at the centre of leisure parks or erected in the context of festivals. Being seen, being recognized and sometimes accepted into a group are essential aspects of contemporary jumping activities. Put simply, the actual experience is therefore not completely individualized, to the extent that jumpers will almost always be accompanied by friends or family who witness the jump (and/or who participate in it as well). It must also be noted how jumpers frequently share their accomplishment with a potentially global readership via Tweets, blogs or YouTube films. Of course, it must at the same time be stressed how the social reference group ‘participating’ in the event is individually selected and/or created; and the entire undertaking does of course remain voluntary. Sports and extreme sports may on this score be very different from the performance of other genres of activity, like the writing of poetry and painting, genres that are indeed tied to an essentially solitary experience, but which Turner lumps into the same category. In other words, it is often the case that the performance of extreme sports takes on highly ritualistic forms, although clearly no longer within a religious framework. Contemporary bungee jumping may even, in some cases, replicate some even more evident aspects of a ‘classical’ rite de passage. Daring activities, such as bungee jumping, are sometimes arranged on stag nights or bachelor parties. This is the time when a man who is about to get married celebrates the future event by spending an action packed day with friends – and sometimes bungee jumping for the groom- to-be is a central part of the programme. The celebration on the stag night marks and initiates the coming of his status-transformation: from unmarried, to married. Liminality and the Modern 188 On the other hand, Turner’s suggestions may prove to be overly ‘positive’ concerning the innovative nature of what he calls the ‘liminoid’. In his depictions of the liminoid Turner includes such an array of activities (sports, literature, study at universities, fine arts) that not only does their joint categorization become analytically problematic, but so also does his normative assumption, most often implicit, that such activities are both creative and playful in a positive sense. The jumping may in many cases be connected to no element of self-reflection whatsoever. It is hard to see how the performance of extreme sports could ever effect substantive changes that have an impact on the social and moral order. While bungee jumping clearly retains ritual elements, and more so than Turner’s framework allows us to recognize, the practice of bungee hardly involves those features of ‘anti-structure’ that Turner claims still to be present in the liminoid. Bungee jumping may contain very little or perhaps even nothing of that reflexivity and scrutiny that leads to innovation or heightened consciousness of roles, a reflection of the links that tie individuals to society. It is for this reason that one might instead invoke the term ‘limivoid’. We invoke this term as an analytical tool, as it still contains the notion of boundary and limit experiences (limes) but ties it to the experiential void that seems to go with such experiences in ‘extreme sports’. It is a feeling of danger, a culture-specific elaboration of falling or flying, and clearly a deliberately provoked near-death experience. It is a jump into the abyss: a jump into the void – and that is it. It is fun, of course, a short laugh, a moment of playful encounter with death; and then back to normality. Our argument is not moralistic; people are of course free to have a good time and ‘feel the adrenaline’ when jumping from a crane on an elastic chord. Nor are we suggesting that ‘pre-modern’ rituals were always only religious and therefore ‘good’ and positively meaningful. The very likely connection between the volador ritual and human sacrifice should suffice to dismantle any such romantic idea. What it is possible to argue is that just as pre-modern practices of flying rituals were deeply tied to an experience embedded within symbolic universes and linked to personality transforming ordeals, so have modern extreme sports, and the larger entertainment industry built around them, become tied to an invocation of liminal experience that is essentially void of experiential substance. This also means that the in-between experience itself has undergone a radical transformation: the ‘other side’ of the experience is no longer a transformed or re-generated human being; neither is it an entry into another season or period of a cosmological calendar. The jump is into a void which is simply ... a bottomless void with no other meaning. The other side of the experience is quite simply ‘death’, a death which is of course not real, but which is ‘nearly’ felt, just for the fun of it. And once it has been nearly felt, the subject returns to normal. There is no ‘other’ on the other side. And that is also why the adrenalin-filled experience leaves the jumping subject only half-satisfied, still craving for more, constantly pushing the limits for more excitement, crossing yet another boundary; and this search itself is, alas, limitless; exactly like gambling. From Liminal to Liminoid to Limivoid 189 Searching for Experience in the Constant Breaking of Boundaries We live in a world hungry for experience; and trying to satisfy this hunger, we seek for ever more ‘kicks’ (Boeve 1999). We are fascinated by the boundary, and the tourism and leisure industries thrive and make big money on that fascination. This is not just an epiphenomenal, curious aspect of life today, a distraction from economic and political realities and structures; rather, it takes us to the heart of contemporary modernity.
